Abstract
text

THIS TEMPLATE IS DESIGNED TO HELP GUIDE YOUR PROJECT DEVELOPMENT. YOU CAN CHANGE OR ADD ANY SECTIONS, BUT I NEED A BRIEF BACKGROUND (WHAT IS YOUR QUESTION, WHY, WHAT DOES THE DATA LOOK LIKE?). THEN YOU NEED TO IMPORT THE DATA, CHECK IT, PERFORM EDA, RUN AND INTERPRET TESTS, DEVELOP APPROPRIATE PLOTS, AND THEN BRIEFLY SUMMARIZE YOUR FINDINGS.

This template also introduces the idea of using quarto to write a reproducible paper. You may need to play with the execute options in R. Most of this follows a paper (Bauer & Landesvatter 2023). Notice this is a citation input from my zotero (which then saved it in this projects references.bib file); more information on citations @ https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/citations.html. The paper is now shown in my references, with a link to the document.

1 Background and metadata

Before diving into the goal of this project, it’s important to provide some context on the sport and why analyzing it is meaningful. Sports betting is a global industry worth over $500 billion, and football (soccer) is at the heart of it — played by over 250 million people across more than 200 countries, making it the most popular sport in the world. Among all leagues, the English Premier League (EPL) stands out as the most-watched and competitive domestic league, drawing fans, analysts, and bettors from around the globe. This global appeal and the high stakes involved make the EPL a valuable subject for data analysis and performance research.

Understanding how player traits relate to value is important in both real-life scouting and sports analytics. Teams and analysts often rely on data like this to make decisions about transfers, contracts, and player development. Although this model simplifies the real world heavily by excluding factors like age, potential, or fame, it still reveals useful insights—specifically that pace and passing are strong, statistically significant predictors of value.

The primary goal of this project is to determine how a soccer player’s on-field attributes—such as pace, shooting, and passing affect their market value. Using statistical modeling, specifically multiple linear regression, I will attempt to identify which performance-related traits are most strongly associated with a player’s estimated monetary worth in the transfer market. The data was obtained from the FIFA 21 Complete Player Dataset on Kaggle, an open-source data platform. This dataset compiles detailed information on over 18,000 professional players, including their skill ratings, demographic information, and financial estimates like market value and wage.

2 Read in (Import) your data

.

player_data <- read.csv("fifa21 raw data v2.csv", stringsAsFactors = TRUE)

4 Exploratory data analysis

library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)


# Clean relevant columns
clean_num <- function(x) as.numeric(sub(" .*", "", x))
player_data$PAC <- clean_num(player_data$PAC)
player_data$SHO <- clean_num(player_data$SHO)
player_data$PAS <- clean_num(player_data$PAS)

# 4. Convert Value to numeric (in euros)
convert_value <- function(val) {
  val <- gsub("€", "", val)
  val <- gsub("K", "e3", val)
  val <- gsub("M", "e6", val)
  val <- gsub("B", "e9", val)
  as.numeric(eval(parse(text = val)))
}

player_data$ValueEUR <- sapply(player_data$Value, convert_value)


playerdata_clean <- player_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(PAC), !is.na(SHO), !is.na(PAS), !is.na(ValueEUR))
library(tidyr)
playerdata_clean<- separate_wider_delim(player_data, Positions,  ",", names=c("primary_position", NA, NA),too_few="align_start")
ggplot(playerdata_clean, aes(x = primary_position, y = ValueEUR)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightgreen") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(title = "Market Value by Position", x = "Position", y = "Market Value (€)") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, size = 8, hjust = 1))
ggplot(player_data, aes(x = PAC, y = ValueEUR)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Pace vs. Market Value", x = "Pace", y = "Market Value (€)")

5 Run statistical tests

Null hypothesis H₀: There is no significant relationship between player attributes (Pace, Passing, and Shooting) and their market value.

Alternative hypothesis H₁: At least one of the player attributes (Pace, Passing, or Shooting) has a significant effect on market value.

value_model <- lm(ValueEUR ~ PAC + SHO + PAS, data = playerdata_clean)

plot(value_model)

Assumptions mostly met, not perfectly, but we have to account for the fact that the bigger the estimate/ variance = more noise, so for the sake of this report, it will do.

summary(value_model)

Call:
lm(formula = ValueEUR ~ PAC + SHO + PAS, data = playerdata_clean)

Residuals:
      Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
-10924233  -2924951  -1385212    952143 174859741 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -17932424     386399 -46.409   <2e-16 ***
PAC             70748       5114  13.833   <2e-16 ***
SHO              8058       5039   1.599     0.11    
PAS            270358       6824  39.621   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 7023000 on 18975 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.1649,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.1648 
F-statistic:  1249 on 3 and 18975 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16
value_model2 <- lm(ValueEUR ~ PAC + PAS, data = playerdata_clean)

plot(value_model2)
summary(value_model2)

Call:
lm(formula = ValueEUR ~ PAC + PAS, data = playerdata_clean)

Residuals:
      Min        1Q    Median        3Q       Max 
-11008139  -2927721  -1388317    962444 174937631 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) -18003443     383854  -46.90   <2e-16 ***
PAC             72333       5018   14.41   <2e-16 ***
PAS            277203       5314   52.16   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 7024000 on 18976 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.1648,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.1647 
F-statistic:  1872 on 2 and 18976 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

6 Publication-quality graphs

ggplot2 is your friend here! Remember to add in labels for axes (with units!), a good title, and pick the right graph type! Examples below for a few common needs using the iris dataset

ggplot(player_data, aes(x = ValueEUR)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10(labels = scales::dollar_format(prefix = "€")) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Player Market Value",
    x = "Market Value (log scale)",
    y = "Number of Players"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
# Add predicted values to the data
player_data$Predicted_Value <- predict(value_model2)

# Plot
library(scales)  # for comma format
ggplot(player_data, aes(x = ValueEUR, y = Predicted_Value)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Player Market Value",
    x = "Actual Market Value (€)",
    y = "Predicted Market Value (€)"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(player_data, aes(x = PAS, y = ValueEUR)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, color = "darkgreen") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Effect of Passing on Market Value",
    x = "Passing Score",
    y = "Market Value (€)"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(player_data, aes(x = PAC, y = ValueEUR, color = PAS)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_color_gradient(low = "lightblue", high = "darkblue") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Player Market Value by Pace and Passing",
    subtitle = "FIFA 21 data — color indicates passing ability",
    x = "Pace Rating",
    y = "Market Value (€)",
    color = "Passing"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

7 Brief discussion

This analysis explored how a soccer player’s individual attributes—specifically pace and passing—influence their market value. The dataset is quite large, containing over 18,000 players and many variables. Therefore, for the sake of my project I focused on just a few key attributes to keep the scope manageable and to demonstrate my ability to apply regression techniques. Using data from the FIFA 21 player dataset and a multiple linear regression model, I found that both pace and passing were statistically significant predictors of a player’s value, while shooting was not and was removed from the final model.

The final model showed that higher scores in pace and passing are strongly associated with higher estimated transfer values. This makes intuitive sense, as these skills are critical across many field positions and are likely to influence both real-world performance and perceived potential in the FIFA dataset. This was also made very clear visually through the graphs used for exploratory data analysis and my final graphs.

While the model does a good job of identifying general trends, there are of course limitations. For example, it doesn’t account for factors like age, club popularity, or player reputation—non-skill traits that can greatly influence market value in real-world scenarios. Future analyses could incorporate these variables for a more robust model.

Overall, this project demonstrates how statistical analysis can reveal the measurable impact of specific skills on player valuation in professional soccer, and highlights the usefulness of regression techniques in sports analytics.

8 Notes on rendering

When you use the render menu button, Rstudio only produces the first format (here, the html). To get everything, you can go to the the terminal tab (next to the console tab in bottom left window) and type

quarto render report_template.qmd

that will produce all listed formats (here, html, docx, and pdf).

References

Bauer PC, Landesvatter C (2023) Writing a reproducible paper with RStudio and Quarto.
